name: git-workspace-init
description: Initialize a new git worktree and branch for feature development or bug fixes. Use when: (1) Starting work on a new feature, (2) Beginning a bug fix, (3) Creating an isolated workspace for any task, (4) You want to work in parallel on multiple branches. This skill handles branch naming with conventional branch conventions, worktree creation, and remote push setup.
capabilities: Generate conventional branch names from task type and description
Create isolated git worktrees in .worktrees directory
Set up remote tracking branches automatically
Support 10 task types: feat, fix, hotfix, docs, refactor, test, chore, perf, ci, style
Apply naming rules: lowercase, hyphens, no special characters
Provide cleanup instructions after PR merge
author: BillChirico
securitySummary: This is a documentation-only skill containing workflow definitions and example code blocks. All subprocess examples use hardcoded command arrays with no user input injection risk. The 'C2 keywords' and 'weak crypto' static findings are false positives triggered by benign words in documentation text. No network calls, no file access beyond user's git repository, no external dependencies.
valueStatement: Managing multiple branches and features in parallel is challenging. This skill creates isolated git worktrees automatically using conventional branch naming, enabling clean parallel development without stashing or switching branches.
userTitle: Initialize Git Worktrees for Isolated Development
limitations: Requires existing git repository with configured remote
User must manually navigate to created worktree directory
No automatic branch deletion after merge
Does not handle merge conflicts or rebasing

useCases.title: Parallel feature development
useCases.description: Work on multiple features simultaneously without stashing changes or switching branches repeatedly.

useCases.title: Quick bug fix isolation
useCases.description: Create an isolated workspace for production hotfixes without disturbing current development state.

useCases.title: Documentation updates
useCases.description: Maintain clean separation between code changes and documentation updates using dedicated branches.

bestPractices: Use descriptive task descriptions that clearly indicate the work being done
Choose the appropriate task type (feat, fix, hotfix, etc.) for proper branch categorization
Clean up worktrees after merging PRs using the provided cleanup commands
antiPatterns: Using spaces or uppercase letters in branch descriptions breaks naming conventions
Creating worktrees without pushing to remote loses branch tracking benefits
Leaving unused worktrees accumulate in .worktrees directory over time

faq.question: What is a git worktree?
faq.answer: Git worktrees let you check out multiple branches simultaneously in separate directories without stashing changes.

faq.question: Where are worktrees created?
faq.answer: Worktrees are created in a .worktrees folder at your repository root for organized storage.

faq.question: Can I use this with any git hosting service?
faq.answer: Yes, it works with GitHub, GitLab, Bitbucket, or any git remote that accepts standard git push operations.

faq.question: Is my data safe?
faq.answer: This skill only operates within your existing git repository. No external data access or network calls to third parties.

faq.question: What if the branch already exists?
faq.answer: The skill will report an error. Choose a more specific description or check if work is already in progress.

faq.question: How is this different from git checkout -b?
faq.answer: Git worktrees create isolated directories for parallel work, while checkout -b switches your current directory context.
